PAPER TITLE: Group-Aware Graph Neural Network for Nationwide City Air Quality Forecasting
TECHNICALITIES: City graph plus city-group graph, differentiable grouping network, group correlation encoding, self-attention sequence encoder, message passing between cities and city groups
METHODOLOGY: Historical AQI and weather sequences are encoded with self-attention → cities are softly assigned into latent groups → group correlations are learned to form a city-group graph → message passing is performed on both city and group graphs → decoder forecasts future AQI
DATA SOURCE: China dataset: 209 cities with AQI and weather data; US dataset: 175 counties with PM2.5 and weather data
FORECAST HORIZONS: 6-hour forecasting horizon (using previous 24 hours as input); also evaluated at longer horizons
KEY RESULTS: GAGNN outperforms existing forecasting models on both China and US nationwide datasets and shows especially strong long-term forecasting performance by capturing latent dependencies between distant but correlated cities
LIMITATIONS: Relies on learned grouping hyperparameter and hierarchical graph design; performance depends on quality of AQI and weather data and added model complexity

METHODOLOGY: Hybrid framework: inputs (emissions proxies, meteorology, land use, satellite data) → multilevel graph convolutions simulate pollutant transport → residual deep network models transformation/deposition → PDE-based loss enforces physical consistency → outputs fine-scale pollutant predictions
DATA SOURCE: China nationwide dataset: 1604 monitoring stations, 6 pollutants + satellite (MODIS, OMI) + reanalysis (MERRA2-GMI) + meteorological and land data
FORECAST HORIZONS: Not classic forecasting horizons; focuses on spatial-temporal estimation and reconstruction (daily predictions)
KEY RESULTS: Achieves 11–22% R² improvement and 12–35% RMSE reduction vs baselines; produces physically consistent predictions and superior generalization to unseen locations; strong correlation (0.76–0.92) vs ground truth
LIMITATIONS: PDE assumptions simplified (2D, no vertical convection); depends on proxy variables for emissions; high model complexity and computational cost; dataset partly reliant on external sources and preprocessing
APPLICATION CONTEXT: Fine-scale air quality assessment, environmental monitoring, policy-making, public health exposure estimation

METHODOLOGY: Two-branch Neural ODE: (1) physics branch using open-system diffusion-advection equations; (2) data-driven residual branch; temporal alignment and fusion of both branches for city- and national-scale PM2.5 prediction

PAPER TITLE: Learning with Calibration: Exploring Test-Time Computing of Spatio-Temporal Forecasting (ST-TTC / SD-Calibrator)
TECHNICALITIES: Spectral Domain Calibrator (SD-Calibrator) applied post-hoc to any backbone model (AirFormer, STGCN, etc.); real-to-complex Fast Fourier Transform (rFFT) to identify phase shifts and amplitude fluctuations; iterative test-time adaptation using incoming test data; no full re-training required
METHODOLOGY: Backbone model (e.g. AirFormer) generates initial forecast → rFFT applied to output in spectral domain → phase and amplitude corrections estimated from test data distribution → calibrated forecast produced iteratively → adapts to seasonal shifts or policy-driven emission changes
DATA SOURCE: Evaluated on spatio-temporal forecasting benchmarks including air quality datasets
KEY RESULTS: Robust adaptation to evolving patterns (seasonal shifts, policy changes) without retraining; improves over non-calibrated backbone; plug-and-play compatibility with existing models
LIMITATIONS: Calibration quality depends on sufficient test data for spectral estimation; adds inference latency; limited evaluation specifically on PM2.5 datasets in the paper
APPLICATION CONTEXT: Real-world air quality forecasting deployment where distribution shifts occur due to seasonal changes or emission policy interventions

PAPER TITLE: Can deep learning beat numerical weather prediction?
MODEL TYPE: Deep Learning vs NWP Comparative Review
TECHNICALITIES: Comparative analysis of NWP systems (WRF-Chem, CMAQ, IFS) versus deep learning architectures; evaluation of nowcasting capabilities; analysis of computational requirements and spatial resolution trade-offs
METHODOLOGY: Systematic comparison of NWP and DL approaches across benchmark meteorological and air quality forecasting tasks → identification of scenarios where DL outperforms NWP → analysis of hybridization strategies (DL post-processing of NWP outputs)
DATA SOURCE: Multiple benchmark meteorological and weather datasets; ERA5 reanalysis; precipitation radar data
VALIDATION METRICS: RMSE, MAE, CSI, skill scores relative to NWP baselines
KEY RESULTS: DL models can match or outperform NWP for nowcasting horizons; NWP still superior for medium-range forecasts; DL as post-processing of NWP doubles resolution and corrects biases; computational inference costs drastically lower for DL
LIMITATIONS: DL lacks physical interpretability; requires large training datasets; generalization to unseen meteorological regimes limited

PAPER TITLE: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization
TECHNICALITIES: Adaptive learning rates per parameter; first moment (mean gradient) and second moment (uncentered variance) estimation; bias correction for initial time steps; hyperparameters β1=0.9, β2=0.999, ε=10^{-8}; AdaGrad and RMSProp generalization
METHODOLOGY: Compute gradient of loss → update first moment m_t = β1·m_{t-1} + (1-β1)·g_t → update second moment v_t = β2·v_{t-1} + (1-β2)·g_t² → bias correction m̂_t, v̂_t → parameter update θ_t = θ_{t-1} - α·m̂_t/(√v̂_t + ε)
DATA SOURCE: Various deep learning benchmark tasks: MNIST, IMDB, multi-box object detection
KEY RESULTS: Adam converges faster than SGD and AdaGrad for sparse gradient problems; standard optimizer for training all spatiotemporal deep learning models including GCN-LSTM architectures; handles non-stationary objectives common in air quality time series
LIMITATIONS: May converge to suboptimal solutions vs SGD with momentum on some tasks; weight decay interaction requires AdamW correction
APPLICATION CONTEXT: Standard training optimizer for all models in thesis and literature; cited for training methodology
LATEX BIBTEX CITATION: @inproceedings{kingma2015adam,   author    = {Kingma, Diederik P. and Ba, Jimmy},   title     = {Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization},   booktitle = {International Conference on Learning Representations},   year      = {2015},   url       = {https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.6980} }

PAPER TITLE: A Learning Algorithm for Continually Running Fully Recurrent Neural Networks
TECHNICALITIES: Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL) algorithm; complete gradient computation for fully recurrent networks; teacher forcing training regime where ground-truth previous outputs are fed as decoder inputs during training rather than model predictions
METHODOLOGY: Derive exact gradient computation for fully recurrent networks via RTRL → formulate teacher forcing as training strategy: feed target y_{t-1} as input to decoder at step t instead of predicted ŷ_{t-1} → analyze convergence and exposure bias implications
DATA SOURCE: Synthetic recurrent network benchmarks
KEY RESULTS: Coined 'teacher forcing' training paradigm used in all Seq2Seq and encoder-decoder PM2.5 multi-step forecasting models; enables stable training of recurrent decoders; leads to exposure bias during inference addressed by scheduled sampling
LIMITATIONS: Creates exposure bias (train/test distribution mismatch); scheduled sampling and professor forcing developed as remedies
APPLICATION CONTEXT: Teacher forcing used in thesis multi-step decoder training; foundational reference for Seq2Seq training methodology in AQF models

PAPER TITLE: STGATN: A novel spatiotemporal graph attention network for predicting pollutant concentrations at multiple stations
MODEL TYPE: Spatiotemporal Graph Attention Network (STGATN) — GAT + TCN + Transformer Attention, Encoder-Decoder
TECHNICALITIES: ST-Block: Graph Attention Network (GAT) for dynamic spatial dependencies + Temporal Attention (TemAtt) for nonlinear temporal correlations + Fusion Gate for adaptive integration; Gated Temporal Convolutional Network (GTCN) with dilated causal convolutions for causal temporal feature extraction; Transformer Attention (TransAtt) between encoder and decoder to map historical representations directly to future steps without autoregressive decoding; Spatiotemporal Embedding (STE) encoding positional + timestamp information; Encoder-decoder architecture; 85,827 parameters; hidden dim D=32, 4 attention heads, L=1 ST-Block layer; MAE loss with L2 regularization; Adam optimizer lr=0.001; batch=32; 50 epochs
METHODOLOGY: Model the 12-station Beijing monitoring network as a weighted undirected graph → encode historical P=12 steps through Conv1D projection → encoder (ST-Block + GTCN + Fusion Gate) extracts spatiotemporal correlations → TransAtt converts encoder output directly to Q=6 future representations (bypassing dynamic decoding to avoid error accumulation) → decoder reconstructs future representations → FC layer outputs 6-step PM2.5 predictions; trains on 50%/25%/25% train/val/test split; Mean-Std normalization; PM2.5-only input (no meteorological variables or other pollutants — ablation shows adding them consistently degrades performance)
DATA SOURCE: 12 Beijing monitoring stations (Beijing Environmental Monitoring Center): Aotizhongxin, Changping, Dingling, Dongsi, Guanyuan, Gucheng, Huairou, Nongzhanguan, Shunyi, Tiantan, Wanliu, Wanshouxigong — same 12-station PRSA dataset as UCI Beijing Multi-Site Air Quality dataset
FORECAST HORIZONS: 1h–6h ahead (primary evaluation); extended to 24h in long-term analysis; direct multi-step decoding (Q=6 steps simultaneously via TransAtt, not autoregressive)
VALIDATION METRICS: RMSE, MAE, IA (Index of Agreement); evaluated per horizon (steps 1–6) and per concentration range (0–50, 50–200, 200–500, 500–800 µg/m³) and per time-of-day segment
KEY RESULTS: 6-step PM2.5: RMSE=34.946, MAE=19.156, IA=0.9490; outperforms all 8 baselines (ARIMA, SVR, LSTM, TCN, T-GCN, MTGNN, AGCRN, GC-SRTCN-L) across all horizons; 25.9% RMSE reduction vs ARIMA, 9.0% vs LSTM, 0.6% vs best baseline GC-SRTCN-L; 1-step: RMSE=18.718, MAE=9.848, IA=0.9865; PM2.5-only input outperforms all multi-pollutant and meteorological combinations; ablation confirms adding NO2 increases 6-step RMSE by 10.4%; lightweight: 85,827 params, 17.5s per 100 training iterations on V100 GPU
LIMITATIONS: Fixed input horizon P=12 (same as thesis); performance degrades for extreme PM2.5 > 300 µg/m³; multi-station synchronous optimization occasionally sacrifices individual station accuracy; future work targets 24h–1week horizons; currently restricted to Beijing dataset; no meteorological inputs used (deliberate design choice but limits physical interpretability)
APPLICATION CONTEXT: Multi-station PM2.5 forecasting across exact same 12 Beijing PRSA stations as thesis; direct multi-step decoding (6-step, same as thesis); PM2.5-only target; GAT spatial modeling — highly relevant as architectural comparison baseline
JOURNAL / VENUE: PLOS ONE (Public Library of Science), Volume 20, Issue 7, July 30, 2025
